questions.question: What's the memory cost of an adjacency MATRIX for a graph with n vertices?
options: O(n²) — regardless of how few edges there actually are. Bad for sparse graphs; great for dense.
questions.explanation: An n×n matrix uses n² bits (or bytes). For n=10⁶, that's 10¹² bits = 125 GB. Adjacency list uses O(n + m) = much smaller for sparse graphs (m << n²). Most real-world graphs (social, road, web) are sparse. Matrix wins only for dense graphs (m approaches n²) or algorithms doing 'for each pair' computations like Floyd-Warshall.

questions.question: What is CSR (Compressed Sparse Row) format?
options: Two arrays: row_starts[i] gives the start of vertex i's neighbors in a flattened neighbors[] array. Cache-friendly contiguous iteration, expensive to mutate.
questions.explanation: CSR is the standard layout for sparse matrices and large static graphs. Neighbors of vertex u live in neighbors[row_starts[u] .. row_starts[u+1]] — contiguous memory, perfect for SIMD or cache prefetching. Adding edges requires shifting; CSR is best for build-once-then-query graphs (road networks, compiled web crawl, scientific computing).

questions.question: When does adjacency MATRIX beat adjacency list?
options: Dense graphs (m close to n²) and algorithms with 'for each pair' loops like Floyd-Warshall (all-pairs shortest path) or transitive closure. O(1) edge lookup also wins for some algorithms.
questions.explanation: Floyd-Warshall: for k = 1..n, for i = 1..n, for j = 1..n. Each access is dist[i][j] — natural fit for a matrix. Doing this in adjacency list would require has_edge(i, j) lookups → O(deg) each. Matrix gives O(1). Similarly: transitive closure on a small graph. Trade memory for query time. Only viable for small or dense graphs.

questions.question: What's the BFS complexity per edge for adjacency list vs matrix on a SPARSE graph?
options: Adjacency list: O(m) total — visits each edge once. Matrix: O(n²) — scans every row exhaustively. For sparse graphs (m << n²), matrix is dramatically slower despite both being 'BFS'.
questions.explanation: BFS visits each edge once. Adjacency list iterates only the actual neighbors → O(degree) per vertex → O(m) total. Matrix has no way to know which entries are 1 without scanning all n → O(n) per vertex → O(n²) total. For n=10⁶, m=10⁷: matrix BFS does 10¹² ops; list does 10⁷. Difference of 100K×. The lesson's main.c measures 52× slowdown on a 1000-node graph.

questions.question: Why do production HPC and GPU graph libraries use CSR?
options: Cache-friendly contiguous memory enables SIMD, prefetch, GPU memory coalescing. Mutation is expensive but irrelevant for static algorithms (PageRank, shortest paths on a fixed graph).
questions.explanation: GPU memory loads are 128-byte transactions; CSR's contiguous neighbor lists fit this perfectly. CPU SIMD instructions like AVX-512 want 32-element aligned arrays — CSR's neighbors[] qualifies. Boost.Graph, GraphBLAS, cuGraph, gunrock all rely on CSR. The cost: rebuilding CSR on edge insert is O(m). Pattern: use adjacency list while building, freeze to CSR before running algorithms.

questions.question: Why does NetworkX (Python) use dict-of-dicts despite being slow?
options: Maximum flexibility: each edge/vertex can carry arbitrary attribute dicts. Trade 10-50× perf for easy API. petgraph (Rust), Boost.Graph (C++) trade flexibility for speed.
questions.explanation: NetworkX is the 'pandas of graphs' — maximally pliable for prototyping and analysis. Want to add a string attribute per edge? Just do `g[u][v]['color'] = 'red'`. The cost: dict lookups, Python object overhead, no cache locality. For production graph processing (millions of edges), people switch to igraph (C-backed) or graph-tool (C++-backed). NetworkX is for the exploratory phase.
